narrative_summary:
The MSH2 p.Met1Leu (Met1Leu) substitution affects the initiator methionine of the mismatch‑repair gene MSH2 and has been reported in the context of Lynch syndrome–associated malignancies such as colorectal, ovarian and sebaceous carcinoma[PMC5951856][PMC10589993].
Clinical reports describe the variant in several settings. It was identified in patients with Lynch syndrome who did not fulfil Amsterdam II or revised Bethesda criteria, suggesting a possible disease association[PMC10589993]. In a cohort selected by microsatellite instability, the same change was listed among pathogenic or likely‑pathogenic MSH2 alterations[PMC8864140]. Conversely, a 2019 re‑analysis re‑classified the variant from likely pathogenic to a variant of uncertain significance, and other studies of ovarian cancer and pediatric therapy‑related myeloid neoplasms have reported it as uncertain or VUS[PMC6355179][PMC3493867][PMC7880998]. ClinVar entries derived from these data also label the change as uncertain significance[PMC10010347].
Functional interrogation provides a contrasting perspective. A high‑throughput multiplexed assay of MSH2 missense variants classified p.Met1Leu as functionally neutral, supporting a benign interpretation[PMC7820803]. No additional experimental evidence for loss of function was reported in other functional studies[PMC9545740].
Computational predictions and population data are limited. In silico assessments from the functional screen and a supplementary lung‑cancer dataset described the variant as predicted neutral or benign[PMC7820803][PMC9325712]. No appreciable allele frequency in large reference cohorts was presented, and the variant is absent from the gnomAD summary in the extracted tables.
Taken together, the evidence is discordant: clinical observations have at times treated p.Met1Leu as pathogenic, whereas functional assays and computational predictions consistently indicate a benign effect. The preponderance of recent functional data and the lack of robust segregation or population frequency support a classification of uncertain significance, leaning toward benign, but definitive pathogenicity cannot be confirmed without further clinical correlation.

article_mentions.title: Diagnosis of patients with Lynch syndrome lacking the Amsterdam II or Bethesda criteria

article_mentions.title: The effects of genomic germline variant reclassification on clinical cancer care

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The retrospective study of 854 colorectal cancer patients found that 32 of 100 selected cases carried pathogenic or likely pathogenic MMR gene variants, with microsatellite instability testing being the most effective selection strategy for identifying Lynch syndrome carriers.
article_mentions.title: Impact of Different Selection Approaches for Identifying Lynch Syndrome-Related Colorectal Cancer Patients: Unity Is Strength

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study found that fewer than 1% of women with ovarian cancer carry germline mutations in mismatch repair genes, with only two pathogenic MSH2 mutations identified, including p.Met1Leu.
article_mentions.title: Frequency of mutations in mismatch repair genes in a population-based study of women with ovarian cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: Sequential IHC with MLN1 promoter methylation analysis followed by MSI was identified as the most sensitive (92.3%) and specific (97.7%) screening strategy for Lynch syndrome in women with ovarian cancer.
article_mentions.title: Performance characteristics of screening strategies to identify Lynch syndrome in women with ovarian cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identifies the MSH2 p.Met1Leu variant in a pediatric therapy‑related myeloid neoplasm and classifies it as a variant of uncertain significance based on ACMG criteria and in‑silico predictions.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study focuses on KRAS‑mutant lung cancer lipid metabolism and only reports the MSH2 p.Met1Leu variant in a supplementary table with a predicted neutral effect, providing no functional or clinical evidence for the variant.
article_mentions.title: Targeting de novo lipogenesis and the Lands cycle induces ferroptosis in KRAS-mutant lung cancer
